Glucose.why: Screen and monitor diabetes, assess glycemic control
Glucose.high: Diabetes, stress response, corticosteroids, Cushing syndrome, pancreatitis
Glucose.low: Insulin excess, liver failure, adrenal insufficiency, sepsis, oral hypoglycemics

BUN.what: Blood urea nitrogen — protein metabolism waste product filtered by kidneys
BUN.why: Assess kidney function and hydration status; BUN:Cr ratio helps differentiate prerenal from intrinsic renal disease
BUN.high: Dehydration, kidney disease, high protein diet, GI bleed, CHF, catabolic states

Creatinine.what: Waste product from muscle creatine metabolism, filtered by glomeruli
Creatinine.why: Primary marker for kidney function; used to calculate eGFR for CKD staging
Creatinine.high: CKD, AKI, dehydration, rhabdomyolysis, ACEi/ARB initiation, high muscle mass
Creatinine.low: Low muscle mass, advanced liver disease, pregnancy

Sodium.what: Primary extracellular cation, maintains osmolality and fluid balance
Sodium.why: Evaluate fluid balance, neurological symptoms; critical for identifying SIADH, DI, adrenal crisis
Sodium.high: Dehydration, diabetes insipidus, hyperaldosteronism, excess sodium intake
Sodium.low: SIADH, diuretics, heart failure, cirrhosis, adrenal insufficiency, psychogenic polydipsia

Potassium.what: Primary intracellular cation, critical for cardiac and muscle cell membrane potential
Potassium.why: Monitor cardiac rhythm risk; essential with diuretic therapy, CKD, and ACEi/ARB use
Potassium.high: CKD, ACEi/ARB, K-sparing diuretics, hemolysis (artifact), acidosis, tissue destruction
Potassium.low: Diuretics (loop/thiazide), vomiting, diarrhea, alkalosis, insulin, beta-agonists

Chloride.what: Major extracellular anion, follows sodium and inversely tracks bicarbonate
Chloride.why: Assess acid-base status; calculate anion gap; help differentiate metabolic acidosis types
Chloride.high: Dehydration, renal tubular acidosis, normal saline excess, hyperparathyroidism
Chloride.low: Vomiting, diuretics, SIADH, metabolic alkalosis, respiratory acidosis compensation

CO2 (Bicarbonate).what: Serum bicarbonate — primary metabolic acid-base buffer
CO2 (Bicarbonate).why: Evaluate metabolic acidosis/alkalosis; essential for anion gap calculation
CO2 (Bicarbonate).high: Metabolic alkalosis (vomiting, diuretics), chronic respiratory acidosis (COPD compensation)
CO2 (Bicarbonate).low: Metabolic acidosis (DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, diarrhea, RTA)

Calcium.what: Essential mineral for bone, nerve conduction, muscle contraction, and coagulation
Calcium.why: Evaluate parathyroid function, bone disease, malignancy; always correct for albumin level
Calcium.high: Primary hyperparathyroidism, malignancy (PTHrP), vitamin D excess, thiazides, sarcoidosis
Calcium.low: Hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, CKD, hypomagnesemia, pancreatitis

Total Protein.what: Combined albumin and globulin in serum
Total Protein.why: Screen for nutritional status, liver synthetic function, and immunoglobulin disorders
Total Protein.high: Dehydration, chronic inflammation, multiple myeloma, HIV
Total Protein.low: Liver disease, malnutrition, nephrotic syndrome, malabsorption

Albumin.what: Major plasma protein synthesized by the liver (half-life ~20 days)
Albumin.why: Assess liver synthetic function, nutritional status; negative acute phase reactant (drops during inflammation)
Albumin.low: Liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition, inflammation, burns, enteropathy

Bilirubin, Total.what: Heme degradation product — sum of direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated)
Bilirubin, Total.high: Hemolysis, hepatitis, biliary obstruction, Gilbert syndrome (benign), cirrhosis

ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase).what: Enzyme found in liver (biliary), bone, intestine, and placenta
ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase).why: Elevated in biliary obstruction and bone disease; use GGT to confirm hepatic origin vs bone
ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase).high: Cholestasis, biliary obstruction, bone disease (Paget, fractures), pregnancy, growing children
ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase).low: Malnutrition, hypothyroidism, pernicious anemia, zinc deficiency

AST (SGOT).what: Aminotransferase enzyme in liver, heart, muscle, and red blood cells
AST (SGOT).why: Liver injury marker; less liver-specific than ALT; AST:ALT ratio >2 suggests alcoholic liver disease
AST (SGOT).high: Hepatitis, cirrhosis, MI, muscle injury, hemolysis, medications (statins, acetaminophen)

ALT (SGPT).why: Most liver-specific enzyme for hepatocellular injury; first-line liver damage screen
ALT (SGPT).high: Hepatitis (viral, drug, autoimmune), fatty liver (NAFLD/NASH), cirrhosis, ischemia

eGFR.what: Estimated glomerular filtration rate calculated from creatinine, age, sex, race
eGFR.why: Stage CKD (G1 ≥90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15); guide drug dosing
eGFR.low: CKD—value directly indicates stage; decline >3 mL/min/year is rapid progression

WBC.what: Total white blood cell count — includes neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils
WBC.why: Detect infection, inflammation, immunosuppression, hematologic malignancy
WBC.high: Bacterial infection, inflammation, stress (demargination), steroids, leukemia, smoking
WBC.low: Viral infection, chemotherapy, bone marrow suppression, autoimmune neutropenia, medications

RBC.what: Red blood cell count — oxygen-carrying cells
RBC.why: Evaluate anemia and polycythemia; interpreted alongside Hgb, Hct, and indices (MCV/MCH)
RBC.high: Polycythemia vera, chronic hypoxia (COPD, high altitude), dehydration
RBC.low: Anemia (iron deficiency, chronic disease, B12/folate, bleeding, hemolysis)

Hemoglobin.what: Oxygen-carrying protein within red blood cells
Hemoglobin.why: Primary marker for anemia diagnosis and severity; guides transfusion decisions
Hemoglobin.high: Polycythemia, dehydration, chronic hypoxia (COPD, OSA), EPO use
Hemoglobin.low: Iron deficiency, chronic disease, bleeding, B12/folate deficiency, hemolysis, thalassemia

Hematocrit.what: Percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells
Hematocrit.why: Parallels hemoglobin; affected by hydration status more than Hgb; rough rule: Hct ≈ 3× Hgb

Platelets.what: Thrombocytes — essential for primary hemostasis and clot formation
Platelets.why: Assess bleeding risk (<50K) and thrombotic risk; monitor heparin therapy (HIT)
Platelets.high: Reactive thrombocytosis (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency), myeloproliferative disorders
Platelets.low: ITP, TTP/HUS, DIC, liver disease/hypersplenism, medications, bone marrow failure, HIT

MCV.what: Mean corpuscular volume — average RBC size in femtoliters
MCV.why: Classify anemia: microcytic (<80 fL), normocytic (80-100), macrocytic (>100); directs workup
MCV.high: B12/folate deficiency, alcohol use, hypothyroidism, liver disease, MDS, reticulocytosis
MCV.low: Iron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease, lead poisoning, sideroblastic anemia

MCH.what: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin — average hemoglobin mass per RBC
MCH.why: Parallels MCV; helps confirm microcytic/hypochromic vs macrocytic/hyperchromic classification

MCHC.what: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration — Hgb concentration per RBC volume
MCHC.why: Elevated MCHC is uncommon and fairly specific for spherocytosis
MCHC.high: Hereditary spherocytosis, severe dehydration, cold agglutinins (artifact)

RDW.what: Red cell distribution width — coefficient of variation of RBC volume
RDW.why: Elevated RDW = anisocytosis (mixed RBC sizes); helps differentiate iron deficiency (high RDW) from thalassemia (normal RDW)
RDW.high: Iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, mixed nutritional deficiency, post-transfusion, MDS
RDW.low: Not clinically significant (narrow size distribution is normal)

Neutrophils %.what: Percentage of WBCs that are neutrophils — first responders to bacterial infection
Neutrophils %.why: Calculate ANC (WBC × %Neut); ANC <1500 = neutropenia, <500 = severe (infection risk); guide empiric antibiotics
Neutrophils %.high: Bacterial infection, steroids (demargination), stress, MI, medications, CML
Neutrophils %.low: Viral infection, medications (chemo, clozapine, methimazole), aplastic anemia, autoimmune

Lymphocytes %.what: Percentage of WBCs that are lymphocytes (T-cells, B-cells, NK cells)
Lymphocytes %.why: Evaluate viral infections, immune status; absolute lymphocyte count used in HIV monitoring
Lymphocytes %.high: Viral infections (EBV, CMV, hepatitis), CLL, pertussis, lymphoma
Lymphocytes %.low: HIV/AIDS, steroids, immunosuppressants, SLE, sepsis (lymphopenia = poor prognosis)

Monocytes %.what: Percentage of WBCs that are monocytes — phagocytes that mature into tissue macrophages
Monocytes %.why: Elevated in chronic infections and inflammatory states; part of differential for leukemias
Monocytes %.high: Chronic infections (TB, endocarditis, fungal), autoimmune disease, recovery phase of acute infection
Monocytes %.low: Bone marrow suppression, hairy cell leukemia

TSH.what: Thyroid-stimulating hormone from anterior pituitary — regulates T4/T3 production
TSH.why: Best initial screening test for thyroid dysfunction; inversely related to thyroid hormone levels
TSH.high: Primary hypothyroidism (Hashimoto), recovery from sick euthyroid, TSH-secreting adenoma (rare)
TSH.low: Hyperthyroidism (Graves, toxic nodule), thyroiditis (subacute, postpartum), exogenous T4 excess

Free T4.what: Unbound thyroxine — biologically active fraction of total T4
Free T4.why: Confirm and classify thyroid dysfunction identified by TSH; not affected by TBG changes (pregnancy, OCP)
Free T4.low: Hypothyroidism, sick euthyroid (nonthyroidal illness), central hypothyroidism

Free T3.what: Unbound triiodothyronine — most potent active thyroid hormone (4× T4 potency)
Free T3.why: Diagnose T3 thyrotoxicosis (elevated T3 with normal T4); last to fall in hypothyroidism
Free T3.high: T3 thyrotoxicosis, early Graves disease, amiodarone type 2
Free T3.low: Sick euthyroid syndrome, severe hypothyroidism, euthyroid with low T3 (common in illness)

HbA1c.what: Glycated hemoglobin — reflects average blood glucose over preceding 2-3 months (RBC lifespan)
HbA1c.why: Diagnose diabetes (≥6.5%), prediabetes (5.7-6.4%); monitor glycemic control; target usually <7% in adults
HbA1c.high: Uncontrolled diabetes, new diabetes diagnosis; falsely high with iron deficiency, splenectomy
HbA1c.low: Hemolytic anemia, blood loss, transfusions, hemoglobin variants (falsely low); also tight control

Fasting Glucose.why: Diagnose diabetes (≥126 mg/dL), prediabetes (100-125); requires confirmation on separate day
Fasting Glucose.high: Diabetes mellitus, stress hyperglycemia, Cushing syndrome, acromegaly, medications (steroids)
Fasting Glucose.low: Insulinoma, exogenous insulin, sulfonylureas, adrenal insufficiency, liver failure, sepsis

Total Cholesterol.what: Sum of LDL + HDL + VLDL cholesterol in blood
Total Cholesterol.why: Initial lipid screen; used in ASCVD risk calculation; less useful alone than LDL/HDL breakdown
Total Cholesterol.high: Familial hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, cholestasis, high-fat diet

LDL Cholesterol.what: Low-density lipoprotein — primary atherogenic cholesterol particle
LDL Cholesterol.why: Primary target for ASCVD risk reduction; statin therapy goals based on risk category
LDL Cholesterol.high: Familial hypercholesterolemia, dietary saturated fat, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome

HDL Cholesterol.what: High-density lipoprotein — reverse cholesterol transport from arteries to liver
HDL Cholesterol.why: Protective against ASCVD; included in pooled cohort risk equation; <40 in men/<50 in women is low
HDL Cholesterol.high: Regular exercise, moderate alcohol, genetics (CETP deficiency), estrogen
HDL Cholesterol.low: Metabolic syndrome, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, anabolic steroids, obesity, hypertriglyceridemia

Triglycerides.what: Glycerol esters of fatty acids — dietary and hepatic origin
Triglycerides.why: ASCVD risk (when >150); pancreatitis risk when >500; monitor in metabolic syndrome
Triglycerides.high: Diet (carbs/alcohol), obesity, diabetes, hypothyroidism, medications (steroids, estrogen, HIV meds)

VLDL.what: Very-low-density lipoprotein — triglyceride-rich particle from liver
VLDL.why: Estimated as TG÷5; contributes to non-HDL cholesterol; atherogenic when remnants accumulate
VLDL.high: Elevated triglycerides, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, familial dyslipidemia

Non-HDL Cholesterol.what: Total cholesterol minus HDL — represents all atherogenic particles (LDL+VLDL+IDL+Lp(a))
Non-HDL Cholesterol.why: Better predictor of ASCVD than LDL alone, especially with high triglycerides; secondary treatment target
Non-HDL Cholesterol.high: Same causes as elevated LDL and triglycerides combined

Iron.what: Serum iron level — represents iron bound to transferrin in circulation
Iron.why: Part of iron panel; interpret with ferritin, TIBC, and TSAT together; fluctuates diurnally
Iron.high: Hemochromatosis, iron overdose, hemolytic anemia, multiple transfusions, liver disease
Iron.low: Iron deficiency (dietary, blood loss, malabsorption), chronic disease (functional iron deficiency)

Ferritin.what: Iron storage protein — reflects total body iron stores; also an acute phase reactant
Ferritin.why: Most sensitive test for iron deficiency (<30 ng/mL diagnostic); may be falsely elevated by inflammation
Ferritin.high: Iron overload (hemochromatosis), inflammation/infection (acute phase), liver disease, malignancy
Ferritin.low: Iron deficiency (most specific early marker); <12 is definitive, <30 likely deficient

TIBC.what: Total iron-binding capacity — indirect measure of transferrin (main iron transport protein)
TIBC.why: High TIBC = body trying to absorb more iron (iron deficiency); low TIBC = chronic inflammation
TIBC.high: Iron deficiency (body upregulates transferrin), pregnancy, OCP use
TIBC.low: Chronic disease/inflammation, malnutrition, liver disease, nephrotic syndrome

Transferrin Saturation.what: Percentage of transferrin occupied by iron (serum iron ÷ TIBC × 100)
Transferrin Saturation.why: Key for hemochromatosis screening (>45% warrants HFE gene testing); <20% consistent with iron deficiency

Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy.what: Calcidiol — circulating storage form of vitamin D (half-life ~3 weeks)
Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy.why: Best indicator of vitamin D status; deficiency (<20) linked to bone disease, falls; insufficiency 20-29
Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy.high: Excess supplementation, granulomatous disease (sarcoid, TB), lymphoma
Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy.low: Insufficient sun exposure, dark skin, obesity, malabsorption, CKD, liver disease, certain medications

Vitamin B12.what: Cobalamin — essential cofactor for DNA synthesis and myelin maintenance
Vitamin B12.why: Diagnose macrocytic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, cognitive changes; check methylmalonic acid if borderline
Vitamin B12.low: Pernicious anemia, strict vegan diet, metformin use, gastric surgery, celiac/Crohn, H. pylori

Folate.what: Vitamin B9 — essential for DNA synthesis, methylation, and neural tube development
Folate.why: Diagnose macrocytic anemia; always check with B12 (treating folate without B12 can worsen neuro damage)
Folate.low: Malnutrition, alcohol use, malabsorption (celiac), pregnancy, hemolytic anemia, phenytoin, methotrexate

Magnesium.what: Second most abundant intracellular cation; cofactor for 300+ enzyme reactions
Magnesium.why: Deficiency causes arrhythmias, seizures, refractory hypokalemia; commonly overlooked
Magnesium.high: Renal failure, excess supplementation, lithium, hypothyroidism
Magnesium.low: Diuretics (loop/thiazide), alcohol use, PPI therapy, diarrhea, malabsorption, DKA

Phosphorus.what: Essential mineral for bone (85%), ATP production, DNA, and cell membranes
Phosphorus.why: Evaluate bone metabolism, renal function; inversely related to calcium (PTH regulation)
Phosphorus.high: CKD (GFR <30), hypoparathyroidism, tumor lysis syndrome, rhabdomyolysis, excess intake
Phosphorus.low: Refeeding syndrome, hyperparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, antacids (bind phosphorus), DKA treatment

Uric Acid.what: End product of purine (DNA/RNA) metabolism, excreted by kidneys
Uric Acid.why: Diagnose gout; monitor tumor lysis syndrome; chronic elevation associated with CKD and CV risk
Uric Acid.high: Gout, CKD, diuretics (thiazide/loop), tumor lysis, high purine diet, myeloproliferative disorders
Uric Acid.low: SIADH, allopurinol/febuxostat therapy, Wilson disease, Fanconi syndrome

PT (Prothrombin Time).what: Time for plasma to clot via the extrinsic pathway (factor VII, X, V, II, fibrinogen)
PT (Prothrombin Time).why: Monitor warfarin therapy; evaluate liver synthetic function; preoperative coagulation screen
PT (Prothrombin Time).high: Warfarin, liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, DIC, factor deficiencies (VII, X, V, II)

INR.what: International Normalized Ratio — standardized PT corrected for reagent variability
INR.why: Standard for warfarin monitoring; target 2.0-3.0 for most indications, 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves
INR.high: Supratherapeutic warfarin, liver failure, vitamin K deficiency, DIC
INR.low: Subtherapeutic warfarin, increased vitamin K intake (dietary or supplements)

PTT (Partial Thromboplastin).what: Time for plasma to clot via the intrinsic pathway (factors XII, XI, IX, VIII)
PTT (Partial Thromboplastin).why: Monitor heparin (UFH) therapy; screen for hemophilia A/B, von Willebrand disease
PTT (Partial Thromboplastin).high: Heparin therapy, hemophilia A (VIII) or B (IX), vWD, lupus anticoagulant, DIC

D-Dimer.what: Fibrin degradation product released when cross-linked fibrin is broken down by plasmin
D-Dimer.why: High negative predictive value — normal D-dimer rules out DVT/PE in low-risk patients; nonspecific when elevated
D-Dimer.high: DVT/PE, DIC, infection/sepsis, surgery, trauma, pregnancy, malignancy, inflammation
D-Dimer.low: Not clinically significant (normal = no active clot breakdown)

Fibrinogen.what: Coagulation factor I — converted to fibrin by thrombin; also an acute phase reactant
Fibrinogen.why: Evaluate bleeding disorders, DIC (consumed → low), inflammation (elevated as acute phase reactant)
Fibrinogen.high: Infection, inflammation, pregnancy, smoking, nephrotic syndrome
Fibrinogen.low: DIC (consumption), severe liver disease, dysfibrinogenemia, massive transfusion

ESR.what: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate — how fast RBCs settle (affected by plasma proteins)
ESR.why: Nonspecific inflammation marker; useful for temporal arteritis, PMR, osteomyelitis monitoring
ESR.high: Infection, autoimmune (RA, SLE, PMR), cancer, anemia, pregnancy, multiple myeloma
ESR.low: Polycythemia, sickle cell, CHF, extreme leukocytosis

CRP.what: C-reactive protein — acute phase reactant produced by liver in response to IL-6
CRP.why: Rises/falls faster than ESR; useful for monitoring infection, autoimmune flares, and treatment response
CRP.high: Bacterial infection, autoimmune flare, tissue injury, post-surgical, malignancy

hs-CRP.what: High-sensitivity CRP — same protein as CRP measured at lower concentrations
hs-CRP.why: ASCVD risk stratification: <1.0 low risk, 1.0-3.0 moderate, >3.0 high risk; guides statin therapy discussion
hs-CRP.high: Higher ASCVD risk; also elevated by acute infection/inflammation (recheck when well)

PSA.what: Prostate-specific antigen — serine protease produced by prostate epithelium
PSA.why: Prostate cancer screening (shared decision-making per USPSTF); monitor after prostatectomy/radiation
PSA.high: Prostate cancer, BPH, prostatitis, UTI, recent ejaculation/DRE, cycling
PSA.low: 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride/dutasteride reduce PSA ~50%; double measured value to estimate true PSA)

Urinalysis pH.why: Evaluate kidney stone risk (uric acid stones in acidic urine, calcium phosphate/struvite in alkaline), RTA, UTI
Urinalysis pH.high: UTI with urease-producing organisms (Proteus), RTA type 1, vegetarian diet, old specimen
Urinalysis pH.low: High protein diet, metabolic acidosis, uric acid stone risk, diarrhea

Urinalysis Specific Gravity.what: Urine concentration reflected by dissolved solute density
Urinalysis Specific Gravity.why: Assess hydration and kidney concentrating ability; >1.030 with preserved kidneys = severe dehydration
Urinalysis Specific Gravity.low: Overhydration, diabetes insipidus, kidney concentrating defect, diuretic use

Urine Protein.why: Screen for kidney disease; dipstick mainly detects albumin; confirm with spot or 24-hour collection
Urine Protein.high: Kidney disease (glomerular or tubular), orthostatic proteinuria (benign), fever, exercise, UTI

Urine Microalbumin.what: Small quantities of albumin in urine not detected by standard dipstick
Urine Microalbumin.why: Screen for early diabetic nephropathy and hypertensive nephropathy; annual screening in diabetes
Urine Microalbumin.high: Diabetic nephropathy, hypertensive nephropathy, early CKD, CHF, fever/exercise (transient)

Microalbumin/Creatinine Ratio.what: Urine albumin normalized to urine creatinine — corrects for urine concentration
Microalbumin/Creatinine Ratio.why: Preferred spot test for albuminuria: <30 normal, 30-300 microalbuminuria, >300 macroalbuminuria
Microalbumin/Creatinine Ratio.high: >30: microalbuminuria (early nephropathy); >300: overt nephropathy; confirm on 2 of 3 samples

GGT.what: Gamma-glutamyl transferase — enzyme in liver, bile ducts, and pancreas
GGT.why: Confirms hepatic origin of elevated ALP (vs bone); sensitive marker for alcohol and biliary disease
GGT.high: Alcohol use (very sensitive), biliary obstruction, medications (phenytoin, barbiturates), fatty liver

LDH.what: Lactate dehydrogenase — ubiquitous intracellular enzyme released during cell damage
LDH.why: Nonspecific marker of tissue damage; useful for hemolysis screen (with haptoglobin, retic count, indirect bili)
LDH.high: Hemolysis, MI, liver disease, pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, malignancy, rhabdomyolysis

Amylase.what: Enzyme produced by pancreas and salivary glands that digests starch
Amylase.why: Classic marker for acute pancreatitis; less specific than lipase (salivary, ovarian sources)
Amylase.high: Acute pancreatitis, salivary gland disease (mumps, stones), renal failure, bowel obstruction
Amylase.low: Chronic pancreatitis (burned-out gland with exocrine insufficiency)

Lipase.why: More specific and sensitive than amylase for pancreatitis; stays elevated longer (8-14 days vs 3-5)
Lipase.high: Acute pancreatitis (>3× upper limit is diagnostic), renal failure, bowel obstruction

CPK.what: Creatine phosphokinase — enzyme in skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle, and brain
CPK.why: Evaluate rhabdomyolysis, statin myopathy, muscle disorders; CK-MB fraction specific to cardiac muscle
CPK.high: Rhabdomyolysis, statin myopathy, vigorous exercise, MI, seizures, intramuscular injections

Troponin I.what: Cardiac-specific contractile protein released when myocardial cells are damaged
Troponin I.why: Gold standard for myocardial infarction diagnosis; serial measurements (0, 3, 6 hours); high-sensitivity assays preferred
Troponin I.high: MI, myocarditis, PE (right heart strain), takotsubo, sepsis, renal failure (chronic low-grade elevation)

BNP.what: B-type natriuretic peptide secreted by ventricular myocytes in response to wall stretch
BNP.why: Diagnose and monitor heart failure; BNP <100 effectively rules out CHF in dyspnea workup
BNP.high: CHF, volume overload, ACS, PE, AF, renal failure, sepsis, ARDS
BNP.low: Obesity (falsely low due to adipose tissue clearance); flash pulmonary edema (may not have risen yet)

NT-proBNP.what: Inactive N-terminal fragment of proBNP — longer half-life than BNP (120 min vs 22 min)
NT-proBNP.why: Same diagnostic utility as BNP for heart failure; renal clearance (higher cutoffs needed in CKD)
NT-proBNP.high: CHF, ACS, CKD (accumulates), AF, sepsis, pulmonary hypertension

Cortisol (AM).what: Primary glucocorticoid from adrenal cortex; follows diurnal rhythm (highest AM, lowest midnight)
Cortisol (AM).why: Screen for Cushing (high) or adrenal insufficiency (low); AM cortisol <3 suggests insufficiency, >18 rules it out
Cortisol (AM).high: Cushing syndrome, stress, exogenous steroids, ectopic ACTH, adrenal tumor
Cortisol (AM).low: Addison disease, secondary adrenal insufficiency, pituitary failure, abrupt steroid discontinuation

DHEA-S.what: Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate — adrenal androgen precursor (stable, no diurnal variation)
DHEA-S.why: Evaluate adrenal androgen excess (hirsutism, virilization); distinguish adrenal vs ovarian source of androgens
DHEA-S.high: Adrenal tumor, PCOS (mild elevation), congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), adrenal Cushing

Testosterone, Total.what: Primary androgen — from testes (95%) in men, ovaries/adrenals in women
Testosterone, Total.why: Diagnose male hypogonadism (2 AM fasting samples <300), PCOS in women; check with SHBG if borderline
Testosterone, Total.high: Exogenous testosterone, PCOS, adrenal or gonadal tumors, CAH
Testosterone, Total.low: Primary hypogonadism (Klinefelter), secondary (pituitary/hypothalamic), aging, obesity, opioids, steroids

Estradiol.what: Primary estrogen — from ovarian follicles in premenopausal women; also aromatized from testosterone
Estradiol.why: Evaluate menstrual disorders, menopause confirmation, fertility monitoring, gynecomastia workup
Estradiol.high: Pregnancy, ovarian tumors/cysts, obesity (aromatization), liver disease, estrogen therapy
Estradiol.low: Menopause, premature ovarian insufficiency, anorexia, intense exercise, hypothalamic amenorrhea

FSH.what: Follicle-stimulating hormone from anterior pituitary — stimulates follicle growth/spermatogenesis
FSH.why: Elevated in menopause/gonadal failure (loss of feedback); low suggests hypothalamic-pituitary cause
FSH.high: Menopause, primary gonadal failure (Turner, Klinefelter), premature ovarian insufficiency
FSH.low: Hypothalamic amenorrhea, pituitary failure, hyperprolactinemia, anabolic steroid use

LH.what: Luteinizing hormone from anterior pituitary — triggers ovulation and testosterone production
LH.why: LH:FSH ratio >2 supports PCOS diagnosis; LH surge triggers ovulation; low suggests central cause
LH.high: Menopause, PCOS (elevated LH:FSH ratio), primary gonadal failure

Prolactin.what: Anterior pituitary hormone — stimulates breast milk production; inhibited by dopamine
Prolactin.why: Evaluate galactorrhea, amenorrhea, infertility; always check before imaging for pituitary mass
Prolactin.high: Prolactinoma, medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, SSRIs), hypothyroidism, chest wall injury
Prolactin.low: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction), dopamine agonist therapy

PTH.what: Parathyroid hormone — regulates calcium by increasing bone resorption, renal reabsorption, and vitamin D activation
PTH.why: Interpret WITH calcium: high Ca + high PTH = primary hyperparathyroidism; low Ca + high PTH = secondary (CKD/VitD)
PTH.high: Primary hyperparathyroidism (adenoma), secondary (CKD, vitamin D deficiency), tertiary (post-transplant)
PTH.low: Hypoparathyroidism (post-surgical, autoimmune), magnesium deficiency (impairs PTH secretion), hypercalcemia of malignancy

Prealbumin.what: Transthyretin — short-half-life protein (2 days) made by liver; transports thyroxine and retinol
Prealbumin.why: More sensitive marker of acute nutritional change than albumin (20-day half-life); useful in ICU/refeeding monitoring
Prealbumin.low: Acute malnutrition, liver disease, inflammation (negative acute phase reactant), hyperthyroidism

Homocysteine.what: Amino acid from methionine metabolism — requires B12, folate, and B6 for clearance
Homocysteine.why: Elevated levels are independent CV risk factor; functional marker of B12/folate deficiency when levels are borderline
Homocysteine.high: B12/folate/B6 deficiency, CKD, hypothyroidism, medications (methotrexate, phenytoin), MTHFR variants

Reticulocyte Count.what: Immature RBCs released from bone marrow — indicates erythropoietic activity
Reticulocyte Count.why: High retics = marrow responding to anemia (hemolysis, blood loss); low retics = marrow failure (production problem)
Reticulocyte Count.high: Hemolytic anemia, acute blood loss, response to iron/B12/folate/EPO treatment
Reticulocyte Count.low: Aplastic anemia, bone marrow suppression, untreated iron/B12/folate deficiency, renal failure (low EPO)

Sed Rate (Westergren).what: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate — same as ESR, alternative nomenclature
Sed Rate (Westergren).why: Nonspecific inflammation screen; normal increases with age (men: age÷2, women: [age+10]÷2)
Sed Rate (Westergren).high: Infection, autoimmune, malignancy, anemia, pregnancy, renal disease
Sed Rate (Westergren).low: Polycythemia, sickle cell disease, CHF, DIC

Lactic Acid.what: Product of anaerobic glycolysis when oxygen delivery is insufficient for aerobic metabolism
Lactic Acid.why: Marker of tissue hypoperfusion and sepsis severity; >4 mmol/L in sepsis = high mortality; guide resuscitation
Lactic Acid.high: Sepsis/shock, tissue hypoxia, mesenteric ischemia, liver failure, seizures, metformin (rare), vigorous exercise

Ammonia.what: Toxic byproduct of protein/amino acid metabolism, normally converted to urea by the liver
Ammonia.why: Elevated in liver failure causing hepatic encephalopathy; correlates poorly with encephalopathy grade
Ammonia.high: Hepatic encephalopathy, cirrhosis, portosystemic shunt, urea cycle defects, Reye syndrome, GI bleed

Direct Bilirubin.what: Conjugated bilirubin — water-soluble form processed by the liver for biliary excretion
Direct Bilirubin.why: Elevated direct bilirubin points to hepatocellular damage or biliary obstruction (vs indirect = hemolysis/Gilbert)
Direct Bilirubin.high: Biliary obstruction (stones, tumor), hepatitis, Dubin-Johnson syndrome, sepsis-associated cholestasis

Haptoglobin.what: Plasma protein that binds free hemoglobin released from lysed RBCs
Haptoglobin.why: Key hemolysis marker: low haptoglobin = consumed by binding free hemoglobin; combine with LDH, retic, indirect bili
Haptoglobin.high: Acute inflammation (acute phase reactant), infection, tissue necrosis
Haptoglobin.low: Intravascular hemolysis (TTP, autoimmune, transfusion reaction, mechanical valves, DIC), ineffective erythropoiesis

Anti-TPO.what: Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase — the enzyme that iodinates thyroglobulin to make T4/T3
Anti-TPO.why: Confirm autoimmune thyroid disease; positive in ~95% Hashimoto, ~70% Graves; predicts progression of subclinical hypothyroidism
Anti-TPO.high: Hashimoto thyroiditis, Graves disease, postpartum thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes (coexisting autoimmunity)

ANA Titer.what: Antinuclear antibodies — autoantibodies directed against nuclear components
ANA Titer.why: Screen for SLE (>95% sensitive) and other connective tissue diseases; low specificity — positive in many conditions
ANA Titer.high: SLE, drug-induced lupus, Sjogren, scleroderma, mixed connective tissue disease, dermatomyositis, RA, healthy elderly
ANA Titer.low: Normal (very sensitive negative result effectively rules out SLE)

RF (Rheumatoid Factor).what: IgM autoantibody against the Fc portion of IgG
RF (Rheumatoid Factor).why: Supports RA diagnosis (70% sensitive); also present in other conditions; higher titers = more aggressive RA
RF (Rheumatoid Factor).high: RA (seropositive), Sjogren syndrome, endocarditis, hepatitis C, cryoglobulinemia, healthy elderly (5-10%)
RF (Rheumatoid Factor).low: Normal; seronegative RA still possible (check Anti-CCP)

Anti-CCP.what: Antibodies against cyclic citrullinated peptides — highly specific for RA
Anti-CCP.why: 95% specificity for RA (vs 70% for RF); can be positive years before clinical RA onset; predicts erosive disease
Anti-CCP.high: Rheumatoid arthritis (seropositive, may be only positive marker in early disease)
Anti-CCP.low: Normal; does not rule out seronegative RA

C3 Complement.what: Complement component 3 — central protein in all complement activation pathways
C3 Complement.why: Low C3 indicates complement consumption by immune complexes; monitor SLE activity and membranoproliferative GN
C3 Complement.low: Active SLE, membranoproliferative GN, subacute bacterial endocarditis, severe liver disease

C4 Complement.what: Classic complement pathway protein — consumed early in immune complex formation
C4 Complement.why: Low C4 + low C3 = active classic pathway consumption (SLE flare); isolated low C4 = hereditary angioedema screening
C4 Complement.low: SLE flare, hereditary angioedema (C1-INH deficiency), cryoglobulinemia, serum sickness

Hemoglobin A1c.what: Glycated hemoglobin — same test as HbA1c (alternate lab naming convention)
Hemoglobin A1c.why: Diagnose diabetes (≥6.5%), prediabetes (5.7-6.4%); monitor glycemic control; target usually <7% in adults
Hemoglobin A1c.high: Uncontrolled diabetes; falsely high with iron deficiency, splenectomy
Hemoglobin A1c.low: Hemolytic anemia, blood loss, transfusions, hemoglobin variants; also very tight glycemic control

C-Peptide.what: Byproduct of proinsulin cleavage — released in equimolar amounts with insulin from beta cells
C-Peptide.why: Distinguish type 1 (low = no beta cell function) from type 2 (normal/high = insulin resistance) diabetes; detect insulinoma
C-Peptide.high: Insulin resistance (type 2 DM), insulinoma, renal failure (decreased clearance)
C-Peptide.low: Type 1 diabetes, late-stage type 2 (beta cell exhaustion), exogenous insulin use (suppresses C-peptide)

Insulin, Fasting.what: Serum insulin level after overnight fast
Insulin, Fasting.why: Quantify insulin resistance (calculate HOMA-IR); diagnose insulinoma (high insulin + low glucose + high C-peptide)
Insulin, Fasting.high: Insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes (early), insulinoma, obesity
Insulin, Fasting.low: Type 1 diabetes, late type 2 diabetes (beta cell failure), surreptitious insulin use

Lipoprotein(a).what: Genetically determined LDL-like particle with apolipoprotein(a) — independently atherogenic
Lipoprotein(a).why: Check once as part of CV risk assessment; >50 nmol/L increases ASCVD risk; not modified by lifestyle or most drugs
Lipoprotein(a).high: Genetic (autosomal co-dominant); increased ASCVD and aortic stenosis risk; consider PCSK9 inhibitor in very high levels

ApoB.what: Apolipoprotein B — one molecule per atherogenic particle (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a))
ApoB.why: Direct measure of atherogenic particle number; better predictor than LDL-C when TG elevated or LDL discordant
ApoB.high: Increased atherogenic particle number, familial hyperlipidemia, metabolic syndrome
ApoB.low: Favorable (fewer atherogenic particles); response to lipid-lowering therapy

Cystatin C.what: Protease inhibitor produced by all nucleated cells — freely filtered by glomeruli
Cystatin C.why: Alternative to creatinine for eGFR; not affected by muscle mass, age, or diet; confirms CKD when creatinine-based eGFR is uncertain
Cystatin C.high: Reduced kidney function, CKD, AKI (rises faster than creatinine in some cases)

IgA.what: Immunoglobulin A — predominant antibody in mucosal secretions (saliva, tears, GI/respiratory mucosa)
IgA.why: Elevated in IgA nephropathy, liver disease; low in selective IgA deficiency (most common primary immunodeficiency, 1:500)
IgA.high: IgA nephropathy, alcoholic liver disease, chronic infections, autoimmune conditions, myeloma (IgA type)
IgA.low: Selective IgA deficiency (usually asymptomatic), common variable immunodeficiency, immunosuppression

IgG.what: Main circulating antibody — provides majority of humoral immune defense
IgG.why: Low levels = recurrent infections; elevated in chronic infections and autoimmune disease; subclass testing for suspected immunodeficiency
IgG.high: Chronic infections, autoimmune disease, liver disease/cirrhosis, IgG myeloma, HIV, sarcoidosis
IgG.low: Common variable immunodeficiency, nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy, immunosuppressants

IgM.what: First antibody produced in acute immune response; pentameric structure trapped in vasculature
IgM.why: Elevated IgM = acute infection (vs IgG = past infection); extreme elevation suggests Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia
IgM.high: Acute infection, Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, primary biliary cholangitis, hepatitis, mononucleosis (EBV)
IgM.low: Common variable immunodeficiency, immunosuppression, protein-losing states
